bedside: Blood glucose (hypoglycaemia is severe malaria criterion)
imaging: ECG prior to anti-malarial therapy (quinine affects QT interval)

management_steps.action: ABCs. Shock: Balsol/Ringers Lactate 20ml/kg bolus. Avoid fluid overload (risk of pulmonary oedema)
Large bore IV line, monitors
Blood glucose, blood gas, blood tests (FBC, U&E, CRP, LFT, malaria smear and antigen, blood cultures)
Type and screen, urine catheter for output monitoring
Regular blood glucose monitoring

Uncomplicated malaria: Co-artem 4 tablets stat, 4 after 8 hours, then BD for 2 days. Alternative: Quinine 10mg/kg PO TDS x 7 days + Doxycycline 3.5mg/kg OD (start day 2) x 7 days. Pregnancy/children: Quinine + Clindamycin
Complicated/severe malaria: First-line Artesunate 2.4mg/kg IV at 0, 12, and 24 hours. Alternative: Quinine 20ml/kg in D5W infused over 4 hours (monitor QT interval) + Clindamycin
ID referral, consider early ICU/HC consult for severe malaria
Observe for vomiting for 1 hour after first dose. If vomiting within 1 hour: re-administer. If persistent: refer Internal Medicine
Malaria is a NOTIFIABLE DISEASE

drugs.adult_dose: 4 tablets stat, 4 after 8 hours, then BD

drugs.notes: Replace doxycycline in pregnancy and children

warnings: Artesunate is first-line for severe malaria - do not delay
Avoid fluid overload - pulmonary oedema is a risk in severe malaria
Monitor QT interval with quinine IV
Hypoglycaemia is both a feature and complication of severe malaria - monitor regularly
Doxycycline starts on DAY 2, not day 1
Observe for vomiting for 1 hour after first dose
Malaria is a NOTIFIABLE DISEASE
protocols.disposition: Uncomplicated: May discharge after observed first dose if no vomiting, with follow-up. Severe: Admit, ICU/HCU if organ dysfunction.
protocols.notes_updates:
Symptoms: Fever, Rigors, Arthralgia, Myalgia, Fatigue, Malaise, Headache, Abdominal pain, Anorexia, Vomiting, Dizziness, Cough
Signs: Fever, Tachycardia, Tachypnoea, Pallor (anaemia), Jaundice (severe malaria), Hepatosplenomegaly, Altered consciousness (cerebral malaria), Signs of shock (algid malaria)
Risk factors: Travel to malaria area, Children, Pregnant and postpartum, Elderly > 65 years, HIV, Previous splenectomy, Non-immune visitors to endemic areas
Investigations - bedside: Blood glucose (hypoglycaemia is severe malaria criterion), Hb (anaemia), U-dipstix, Blood gas (lactate, acidosis)
Investigations - lab: FBC (anaemia, thrombocytopenia), U&E (renal impairment), CRP, LFT (hepatic transaminases), Blood culture, Malaria smear (parasitaemia), Malaria antigen/RDT
Investigations - imaging: ECG prior to anti-malarial therapy (quinine affects QT interval)
Disposition: Uncomplicated: May discharge after observed first dose if no vomiting, with follow-up. Severe: Admit, ICU/HCU if organ dysfunction.
Warnings: Artesunate is first-line for severe malaria - do not delay; Avoid fluid overload - pulmonary oedema is a risk in severe malaria; Monitor QT interval with quinine IV; Hypoglycaemia is both a feature and complication of severe malaria - monitor regularly; Doxycycline starts on DAY 2, not day 1; Observe for vomiting for 1 hour after first dose; Malaria is a NOTIFIABLE DISEASE

imaging: CT as indicated by suspected source

interpretation.meaning: High risk of poor outcome - suspect sepsis

management_steps.action: ABCs, large bore IV access, monitors

Fluid resuscitation: 30ml/kg crystalloid bolus for hypotension or lactate > 4 (within 3 hours). Caution: tailor to patient - generous 20-30ml/kg if hypovolaemic, gentle 10-20ml/kg if euvolaemic, very cautious if fluid overloaded
Obtain blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics if possible (do not delay antibiotics > 1 hour)
Broad spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of recognition
Consider source: community vs nosocomial, likely organism, allergies
Antibiotic selection: Augmentin = good broad spectrum (poor CSF penetration). Atypical pneumonia = add Azithromycin. Meningitis = Ceftriaxone. Soft tissues = Augmentin. Nosocomial = Tazocin
Source control as appropriate to suspected infection
Consider early inotrope (adrenaline) to keep MAP > 65mmHg. Don't wait for fluid bolus to finish before considering inotropes
Consider early intubation if needed (correct hypotension and acidosis first)
Hydrocortisone 100mg TDS if refractory shock
Re-assess: vitals, ABG/VBG (improving acidosis), lactate clearance, urine output > 0.5ml/kg/hr
FASTHUGS assessment

drugs.notes: Good broad spectrum, poor CSF penetration. Community-acquired sepsis.
If refractory shock despite fluids and vasopressors

warnings: Surviving sepsis bundles to be completed within 3 hours: measure lactate, obtain blood cultures prior to AB, administer broad spectrum AB, administer 30ml/kg crystalloid bolus for hypotension/lactate > 4
Within 6 hours: apply vasopressors for MAP > 65, reassess volume status if persistent hypotension or lactate > 4, remeasure lactate
Antibiotics within 1 hour - do not delay for cultures if this will delay antibiotics
Young patients may compensate BP until late - isolated raised lactate may be only sign
Elderly may have isolated hypotension and very non-specific lactate levels
Don't wait for fluid bolus to finish before considering inotropic support
Can initiate vasopressors on peripheral line and change to CVP within 6 hours
High index of suspicion in immunocompromised, DM, elderly, pregnant, paediatric patients
protocols.disposition: ICU consult if requiring continuous inotropes or ventilatory support. Internal Medicine admission for less severe cases.
protocols.notes_updates:
Symptoms: Fever or hypothermia, Rigors, Tachypnoea, Confusion/delirium, Reduced urine output, Malaise, Non-specific symptoms (especially elderly)
Signs: Fever (> 38.3°C) or hypothermia (< 36°C), Tachycardia, Tachypnoea (RR > 22), Hypotension (SBP < 90, MAP < 65), Raised shock index (HR/SBP), Elevated lactate, Altered mental status, Oliguria (< 0.5ml/kg/hr), Skin mottling, Cold peripheries
Risk factors: Immunocompromise, Diabetes Mellitus, Elderly, Pregnancy, Recent surgery, Indwelling lines/catheters, Chronic organ failure, Malignancy
Investigations - bedside: VBG/ABG (lactate, acidosis), Blood glucose, Urine dipstick, Urine βHCG, ECG
Investigations - lab: FBC, U&E, CRP, Lactate, Blood cultures (before antibiotics if possible), INR/PTT, LFT
Investigations - imaging: CXR (pneumonia), Abdominal ultrasound, CT as indicated by suspected source
Disposition: ICU consult if requiring continuous inotropes or ventilatory support. Internal Medicine admission for less severe cases.
Warnings: Surviving sepsis bundles to be completed within 3 hours: measure lactate, obtain blood cultures prior to AB, administer broad spectrum AB, administer 30ml/kg crystalloid bolus for hypotension/lactate > 4; Within 6 hours: apply vasopressors for MAP > 65, reassess volume status if persistent hypotension or lactate > 4, remeasure lactate; Antibiotics within 1 hour - do not delay for cultures if this will delay antibiotics; Young patients may compensate BP until late - isolated raised lactate may be only sign; Elderly may have isolated hypotension and very non-specific lactate levels; Don't wait for fluid bolus to finish before considering inotropic support; Can initiate vasopressors on peripheral line and change to CVP within 6 hours; High index of suspicion in immunocompromised, DM, elderly, pregnant, paediatric patients

peripheral: Intermittent episodes seconds to < 1 min (BPPV) or hours to days (vestibular neuritis)
Auditory findings: tinnitus, hearing loss (Meniere's, labyrinthitis)
central: Mild but can be severe (stroke, MS)

imaging: MRI (if CT negative but high suspicion for posterior circulation stroke)

management_steps.action: Classify type of dizziness: vertigo, near-syncope, disequilibrium, non-specific
Differentiate peripheral vs central vertigo using clinical findings table
Acute vestibular syndrome (continuous vertigo > 24 hours with nausea, vomiting, ataxia, nystagmus): Apply HINTS exam
HINTS exam: Head Impulse Test (normal/negative = central = worrying), Nystagmus (bidirectional/direction-changing = central), Test of Skew (refixation = central)
Any single central HINTS sign = posterior circulation stroke until proven otherwise - urgent CT/MRI
BPPV: Dix-Hallpike to elicit nystagmus, then Epley manoeuvre to reposition canaliths
Meniere's disease: Recurrent attacks with unilateral ear fullness, hearing loss, roaring tinnitus. HINTS may be normal. NB: recurring attacks may represent TIAs/basilar ischemia
Symptomatic treatment: antiemetics, short-term vestibular suppressants
Refer all central causes for neurosurgical/neurology consultation

warnings: A normal (negative) head impulse test is worrying - suggests central cause (posterior circulation stroke)
Any central HINTS sign = stroke until proven otherwise
Do NOT attribute continuous vertigo in elderly/vascular patients to 'inner ear' without ruling out stroke
Meniere's attacks with accelerated frequency may represent impending basilar ischemia/TIAs
Vertebrobasilar insufficiency can cause isolated vertigo without other neurological signs
All patients with acute vestibular syndrome and any central sign need urgent neuroimaging
protocols.disposition: Central vertigo/stroke: Admit. Peripheral vertigo (BPPV, vestibular neuritis): May discharge with follow-up if no red flags.
protocols.notes_updates:
Symptoms: Vertigo (illusion of motion/room spinning), Near-syncope (lightheadedness), Disequilibrium (unsteady gait), Non-specific dizziness
Signs: {
  "peripheral": [
    "Sudden onset",
    "Severe initially, decreasing over time",
    "Intermittent episodes seconds to < 1 min (BPPV) or hours to days (vestibular neuritis)",
    "Torsional/upbeat nystagmus",
    "Induced by head position (BPPV)",
    "No associated neurological findings",
    "Auditory findings: tinnitus, hearing loss (Meniere's, labyrinthitis)"
  ],
  "central": [
    "Gradual or sudden onset",
    "Mild but can be severe (stroke, MS)",
    "Usually continuous weeks/months",
    "Purely vertical, direction-changing, down-beating nystagmus",
    "Little change with head position",
    "Associated neurological findings usually present",
    "Rarely auditory findings"
  ]
}
Risk factors: Hypertension, Diabetes Mellitus, Atrial fibrillation, Previous stroke/TIA, Migraine history, Multiple sclerosis, Trauma, Vestibular neuritis history
Investigations - bedside: HINTS exam (for acute vestibular syndrome), Dix-Hallpike manoeuvre (for BPPV), BP (including orthostatic), HGT, ECG
Investigations - lab: FBC, U&E, Glucose
Investigations - imaging: CT brain (if central cause suspected), MRI (if CT negative but high suspicion for posterior circulation stroke)
Disposition: Central vertigo/stroke: Admit. Peripheral vertigo (BPPV, vestibular neuritis): May discharge with follow-up if no red flags.
Warnings: A normal (negative) head impulse test is worrying - suggests central cause (posterior circulation stroke); Any central HINTS sign = stroke until proven otherwise; Do NOT attribute continuous vertigo in elderly/vascular patients to 'inner ear' without ruling out stroke; Meniere's attacks with accelerated frequency may represent impending basilar ischemia/TIAs; Vertebrobasilar insufficiency can cause isolated vertigo without other neurological signs; All patients with acute vestibular syndrome and any central sign need urgent neuroimaging
